title: Preventing Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia: The Vent Bundle
subtitle: A 64-year-old man is on day 2 of mechanical ventilation in the ICU. The nurse applies the ventilator bundle — head-of-bed elevation, oral care, daily sedation interruption, and aseptic suctioning — to prevent ventilator-associated pneumonia.

patient.name: Howard Pratt
patient.admitDate: Medical ICU, day 2 of mechanical ventilation for acute respiratory failure. Hemodynamically stable on light sedation. Care focused on the ventilator bundle to prevent ventilator-associated pneumonia and support timely extubation.

nursesNote: Howard Pratt, 64, ICU day 2 on mechanical ventilation for respiratory failure, stable on light sedation. Ventilator (VAP) bundle: keep HOB elevated 30-45° unless contraindicated; regular oral care with chlorhexidine; daily sedation interruption (SAT) with a spontaneous breathing trial (SBT) to support earliest safe extubation; hand hygiene and aseptic in-line suctioning; stress-ulcer and DVT prophylaxis; manage circuit condensate away from the patient and avoid unnecessary circuit changes. Monitor for VAP (new infiltrate, fever, leukocytosis, purulent secretions, worsening oxygenation after ≥48 h).
providerOrders: Maintain head of bed at 30-45° unless contraindicated
Oral care with chlorhexidine; aseptic in-line suctioning; hand hygiene
Daily sedation interruption (SAT) with a spontaneous breathing trial (SBT)
Stress-ulcer and DVT prophylaxis per the ventilator bundle
Monitor for VAP; support the earliest safe extubation

opts.text: VAP is pneumonia developing 48 hours or more after intubation
A care bundle reduces VAP risk in ventilated patients
Elevating the head of the bed reduces aspiration and VAP
Oral care lowers harmful oropharyngeal bacteria
An intubated patient does not need oral care
VAP signs include new infiltrate, fever, purulent secretions, and worsening oxygenation
Keep the head of the bed elevated 30-45° unless contraindicated
Daily sedation interruption with a spontaneous breathing trial shortens ventilation
Hand hygiene and aseptic suctioning reduce infection
Stress-ulcer and DVT prophylaxis are part of the ventilator bundle
Routinely changing the ventilator circuit each shift prevents VAP
Earliest safe extubation is one of the best ways to prevent VAP
Perform hand hygiene and keep the head of the bed elevated 30-45°
Provide regular oral care and aseptic suctioning
Perform daily sedation interruption and assess readiness to extubate
Ensure stress-ulcer and DVT prophylaxis per the bundle
Monitor for VAP and extubate at the earliest safe time; document

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Does the action prevent VAP or increase the risk?

opts.text: Keep the head of the bed elevated 30-45°
Perform oral care with chlorhexidine regularly
Daily sedation interruption with a readiness-to-extubate assessment
Use aseptic, in-line (closed) suctioning with hand hygiene
Provide stress-ulcer and DVT prophylaxis per the bundle
Drain ventilator-circuit condensate away from the patient
Keep the patient lying flat to "rest"
Skip oral care because the patient is intubated
Routinely change the ventilator circuit every shift

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Confirm hand hygiene and head-of-bed elevation before care
Deliver oral care and suction using aseptic technique
Coordinate the daily SAT/SBT with the team
Verify prophylaxis and manage circuit condensate safely
Assess for VAP, support extubation readiness, and document
HOB stays elevated and oral care is done — no new infiltrate
Daily SAT/SBT lead to earlier, safe extubation
Aseptic suctioning and hand hygiene keep secretions clear without infection
The patient is weaned and extubated, lowering VAP risk
New fever, purulent secretions, and an infiltrate after 3 days on the vent
Worsening oxygenation with a rising white count
The patient stays oversedated and ventilator days increase
Aspiration occurs because the patient was kept flat

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Safety and Infection Control, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
